title: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism
vignette: A 36-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of a mood disorder. The patient has a history of several depressive episodes that started in her twenties. Two years ago, she was hospitalized after staying up for several nights without sleeping and claiming that she had special powers to "end global poverty and climate change." Her symptoms responded well to treatment and she has remained on the same drug regimen since then. However, the patient now has new-onset constipation, dry skin, and hair loss. She is also concerned about a weight gain of 2.27 kg (5 lb) over the last 3-4 months despite eating healthy, low-calorie foods. Blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg and pulse is 55/min. The patient appears tired, but physical examination is otherwise normal. The diagnosis is established and her current condition is attributed to adverse effects from one of her medications. Which of the following is the most likely culprit drug?

clues.text: staying up for several nights without sleeping and claiming that she had special powers
clues.note: Decreased need for sleep plus grandiose delusions ('special powers to end global poverty') is a manic episode. With prior depressive episodes, this establishes bipolar I disorder - so her chronic 'same drug regimen' is a mood stabilizer.

clues.text: new-onset constipation, dry skin, and hair loss
clues.note: Constipation, dry skin, and hair loss are hypothyroid symptoms. In a bipolar patient on long-term therapy, this triad points to lithium-induced hypothyroidism.
Unintentional weight gain despite low-calorie eating fits a hypometabolic (hypothyroid) state, reinforcing the thyroid etiology of her new symptoms.
Bradycardia (HR 55 / normal 60-100) is consistent with hypothyroidism's slowed metabolism, supporting a drug that causes hypothyroidism - lithium.

whatMatters: She has bipolar I (mania + prior depression) maintained on a mood stabilizer, and now shows classic hypothyroidism (constipation, dry skin, hair loss, weight gain, bradycardia). The mood stabilizer that causes hypothyroidism is lithium - answer E.
strategy: Tie the adverse effect to the right mood stabilizer: lithium -> hypothyroidism, nephrogenic DI, tremor, Ebstein anomaly; valproate -> hepatotoxicity, neural tube defects, weight gain; carbamazepine -> agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia, SIADH, neural tube defects; lamotrigine -> Stevens-Johnson syndrome. Hypothyroid symptoms = lithium.

eliminations.label: carbamazepine - wrong adverse-effect profile
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an anticonvulsant mood stabilizer. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: its hallmark toxicities are agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia and SIADH (hyponatremia), plus neural tube defects - not hypothyroidism. ✅ Correct if: the stem showed cytopenias or hyponatremia.

eliminations.label: citalopram - an SSRI, not a maintenance bipolar drug here
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an SSRI antidepressant. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: SSRIs don't cause this hypothyroid picture and aren't first-line monotherapy maintenance in bipolar I (can precipitate mania). ✅ Correct if: serotonergic side effects (GI upset, sexual dysfunction, hyponatremia) were described.

eliminations.label: clozapine - different toxicities
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an atypical antipsychotic. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it causes agranulocytosis, seizures, myocarditis, and metabolic syndrome - not a hypothyroid triad. ✅ Correct if: the stem flagged neutropenia/agranulocytosis monitoring or myocarditis.

eliminations.label: lamotrigine - SJS, not hypothyroidism
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a mood stabilizer favored for bipolar depression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: its key risk is Stevens-Johnson syndrome/rash, not thyroid dysfunction. ✅ Correct if: a severe mucocutaneous rash were described.

eliminations.label: risperidone - hyperprolactinemia/metabolic, not thyroid
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: an atypical antipsychotic. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: causes hyperprolactinemia, EPS, and metabolic effects, not hypothyroidism. ✅ Correct if: galactorrhea/amenorrhea or extrapyramidal symptoms were present.

eliminations.label: trazodone - sedative/antidepressant, wrong effects
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a serotonin modulator used mainly for insomnia/depression. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: its notable adverse effects are sedation, orthostasis, and priapism - not a hypothyroid syndrome; and it's not the maintenance mood stabilizer here. You chose this. ✅ Correct if: priapism or marked sedation/orthostasis were described.

reasoning: Mania + prior depression = bipolar I on a chronic mood stabilizer
New constipation, dry skin, hair loss, weight gain, bradycardia = hypothyroidism
Among mood stabilizers, lithium causes hypothyroidism
mechanism: Lithium concentrates in the thyroid and inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis and release (impairing iodine uptake and thyroglobulin proteolysis), producing hypothyroidism and sometimes goiter; the compensatory rise in TSH can enlarge the gland. Clinically this gives fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair loss, weight gain, and bradycardia. Management is to add levothyroxine while continuing lithium if it is controlling the mood disorder, with periodic TSH monitoring. Lithium also causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, a fine tremor, and is teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly).

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Psychiatry/Pharmacology > Mood stabilizers: lithium (hypothyroidism, nephrogenic DI, tremor, Ebstein anomaly, narrow therapeutic index), valproate (hepatotoxicity, NTDs, weight gain), carbamazepine (aplastic anemia/agranulocytosis, SIADH, NTDs), lamotrigine (SJS).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two steps: diagnose hypothyroidism from the symptom cluster, then recall which mood stabilizer causes it. The mania history pins the drug class as a mood stabilizer.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on bipolar I vs II, mood stabilizer, hypothyroidism features, nephrogenic DI, agranulocytosis, SIADH? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: You chose G (trazodone). Trazodone isn't a maintenance mood stabilizer and doesn't cause hypothyroidism - its red flags are priapism and orthostasis. The hypothyroid triad maps to lithium.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Memorize the four mood-stabilizer ADR fingerprints; the symptom cluster will point you straight to the drug.

keywords.back: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, fine tremor, Ebstein anomaly (teratogen)
Agranulocytosis/aplastic anemia, SIADH, neural tube defects
Hepatotoxicity, neural tube defects, weight gain, pancreatitis
Add levothyroxine; continue lithium if effective; monitor TSH

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry/Pharm: mechanism, hypothyroidism, nephrogenic DI, tremor, Ebstein anomaly, toxicity/monitoring.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry/Pharm: mechanism, hypothyroidism, nephrogenic DI, tremor, Ebstein anomaly, toxicity/monitoring.

firstAid.detail: Psychiatry: manic episode criteria, maintenance pharmacotherapy.
firstAid.location: Psychiatry: manic episode criteria, maintenance pharmacotherapy.

firstAid.detail: Pharm: valproate, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, clozapine, risperidone, trazodone profiles.
firstAid.location: Pharm: valproate, carbamazepine, lamotrigine, clozapine, risperidone, trazodone profiles.

memoryHook: LITHIUM hits the thyroid (low T), the kidney (nephrogenic DI), and the hands (Tremor). New hypothyroid signs on a mood stabilizer = lithium.
